coredata.dc:title: Defects coordination triggers red-shifted photoluminescence in carbon dots and their application in ratiometric Cr(VI) sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yuecheng
Li, Chengjia
Sun, Lingbo
Zhang, Jie
Yang, Xiaojun
Ma, Hongyan
coredata.dc:description: Hexavalent chromium ion (Cr(VI)) is one of the most severe environmental nonbiodegradable pollutants. Considering its high biological toxicity, it is of great significance to establish a method for Cr(VI) analysis with high sensitivity and reliability. Therefore, a simple and sensitive ratiometric sensor is developed according to the coordination between Cr(VI) and carbon dots (CDs). In the presence of Cr(VI), the original CDs photoluminescence (PL) emission at 445 nm was significantly shifted to 565 nm and gradually intensified. The coordination mechanism was investigated, the results indicated that the dual-amino groups at ortho-position of benzene ring is critical for this coordination. Under the optimal condition, a good linear relationship between the concentration of Cr(VI) and the PL intensity ratio I565/I445 was obtained from 120 nM to 200 µM, with a low detection limit of 37 nM. Moreover, this method is applied into real water samples analysis with a high quality.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Photoluminescence
Ratiometric
Cr(VI)

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 31860089 ); Graduate Education Innovation Program of Yan'an University ( YCX2020007 ); PhD start-up fund of Yan'an University (YDBK2020-30) and Research Program for Youth Project in Yan’an University ( YDQ2018-17 ).

This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31860089); Graduate Education Innovation Program of Yan'an University (YCX2020007); PhD start-up fund of Yan'an University (YDBK2020-30) and Research Program for Youth Project in Yan'an University (YDQ2018-17).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) Illustration of the synthesis procedures of oCDs and their emission properties under 388 nm excitation. b) The PL excitation-emission map of oCDs with and without Cr(VI) coordination. Insets show photographs of the corresponding samples under 388 nm excitation.

a) TEM image (scale bar = 200 nm) and b) high-resolution TEM image of the oCDs (scale bar = 5 nm). c) X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of oCDs. d) FT-IR spectrum of oCDs. e) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of oCDs.

a) Normalized PL emission spectrum with different excitation wavelength of oCDs. b) PL emission spectrum and c) photographs of oCDs with varying concentrations of Cr(VI). d) The trend of PL intensity at 445 nm and 565 nm for the as-prepared oCDs solutions and their intensity ratio I565/I445 in varying concentrations of Cr(VI). e) Hypothesized coordination mechanism and their corresponding energy level diagram.

a) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of oCDs, Cr(VI), as-prepared oCDs-Cr(VI) solution and the summed spectrum of oCDs and Cr(VI). b) Trend of the PL lifetime at 445 nm and 565 nm for the oCDs as-prepared solutions with varying concentrations of Cr(VI).

a) Linear relationship between PL intensity ratio (I565/I445) and concentration of Cr(VI) ranging from 0 to 200 µM. b) The selectivity of this proposed assay towards Cr(VI) over other analytes (concentration of Cr(VI) and other analytes is 50 µM). Insert shows the PL spectrum of oCDs in the presence of different analytes. The error bars represent the standard deviations based on three independent experiment.
Recovery results of the determination of Cr(VI) in tap water and river samples (n = 5).

head.ce:title: Defects coordination triggers red-shifted photoluminescence in carbon dots and their application in ratiometric Cr(VI) sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Analytical Technology and Detection of Yanan, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yan’an University, Yan’an 716000, Shaanxi Province, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Analytical Technology and Detection of Yanan
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Analytical Technology and Detection of Yanan, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yan’an University, Yan’an 716000, Shaanxi Province, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Medical College of Yan'an University, Yan'an 716000, Shaanxi Province, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Medical College of Yan'an University, Yan'an 716000, Shaanxi Province, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hexavalent chromium ion (Cr(VI)) is one of the most severe environmental nonbiodegradable pollutants. Considering its high biological toxicity, it is of great significance to establish a method for Cr(VI) analysis with high sensitivity and reliability. Therefore, a simple and sensitive ratiometric sensor is developed according to the coordination between Cr(VI) and carbon dots (CDs). In the presence of Cr(VI), the original CDs photoluminescence (PL) emission at 445 nm was significantly shifted to 565 nm and gradually intensified. The coordination mechanism was investigated, the results indicated that the dual-amino groups at ortho-position of benzene ring is critical for this coordination. Under the optimal condition, a good linear relationship between the concentration of Cr(VI) and the PL intensity ratio I565/I445 was obtained from 120 nM to 200 µM, with a low detection limit of 37 nM. Moreover, this method is applied into real water samples analysis with a high quality.

ce:section.ce:para: Chromium (Cr) is a kind of heavy metal element, which has been extensively used in the leather staining, electroplating, metallurgic, catalysis and other industries, causing numerous biological and ecological problems [1,2]. Cr(III) and Cr(VI) are the most abundant oxidation states of Cr. Compared with Cr(III), Cr(VI) is evidently more hazardous to human due to its genotoxicity and carcinogenic properties [3]. Thus, many regulations regarding the contents of Cr(VI) have been announced by organizations or governments, e.g. the World Health Organization (WHO) recommends that the concentration of Cr(VI) in drinking water should be less than 50 µg/L. To test the content of Cr(VI), plenty of sensors have been established using inductively coupled plasma mass spectrum (ICP-MS) [4], ion chromatography (IC) [5], atomic absorption spectrum (AAS) [6] and so on. However, these methods rely on large equipment and require tedious and time-consuming sample pretreatment. Beside these methods, photoluminescence (PL) based methods show superior properties owning to its simplicity and high sensitivity.

Carbon dots (CDs), as a new member of PL nanoparticles family, have received considerable attentions since their discovery in 2004. They are becoming the new-generation of luminescent material in comparison with the conventional organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots [7]. In fact, CDs are generally quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles consist of sp2/sp3 carbons, oxygen/nitrogen-based and other modified chemical groups [8]. Due to their numerous merits such as excellent biological compatibility, fascinating optical properties and low toxicity, CDs have received considerable attentions in sensing [9,10], bio-imaging [11,12], drug delivery [13,14] and so on.

Up to now, a series of CDs based sensors have been developed for Cr(VI) analysis [15–17]. Cr(VI) could efficiently quench the luminescence signal from CDs due to the photo-induced electron transfer between CDs surface defects and Cr(VI). However, most of the assessment rely on the quenching signals from single wavelength which made the quantification process fraught with difficulties. The analysis process can be affected by many interferences, including the instrumental parameters, the local concentration of the CDs/Cr(VI), the microenvironment, photobleaching and so on [18]. Ratiometric approach is one of the best ways to overcome these problems. This method relies on the analyte-induced changes from at least two emission bands, which can provide an effective internal reference to increase the sensitivity, stability and accuracy. Herein, it is imperative to develop a reliable ratiometric CDs sensors for Cr(VI) analysis.

In this study, the CDs were prepared via a bottom-up solvothermal approach by using o-phenylenediamine (oPD) as precursor. Typically, oCDs were accomplished by heating oPD in phosphoric acid solution at 200 °C for 24 h in an autoclave and then purified by using 22 µm membrane followed with dialysis. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the oCDs emit blue light under the excitation at 388 nm, subsequently, the emission turned to orange in the presence of Cr(VI). It is known that Cr(VI) can significantly quench the PL emission of CDs, whereas this phenomenon with a red-shifted emission band after Cr(VI) coordination has not been reported before. From the 3D PL emission scanning showing in Fig. 1b, it is clearly a new emission center with longer wavelength is generated which indicate the formation of new defects after Cr(VI) coordination. The coordination mechanism is further investigated and a novel practical Cr(VI) sensor is established based on this phenomenon. This work offers the following advantages: first of all, it is the first time to report that CDs display a red-shifted PL spectrum after Cr(VI) coordination; secondly, the coordination mechanism and the surface structure of CDs are investigated through ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectroscopy, PL lifetime, density functional theory (DFT) calculation and further comparison among several control CDs; thirdly, it provides a sensitive and selective ratiometric Cr (VI) sensor, which can effectively avoid influences from various external factors.

The o-phenylenediamine (oPD), m-phenylenediamine (mPD) and p-phenylenediamine (pPD) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). H3PO4 was brought from Tianjin Yaohua Chemical Reagent Co. LTD (Tianjin, China). The water used in all experiments was ultrapure water from a Milli-Q (18.2 MΩ.cm) system and all other chemicals are analytical grade without further purification.

High Resolution Transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) observations were performed on a FEI Tecnai G2 F20 (FEI). X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) spectrum was carried out with XRD-6100 X (Shimadzu). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was recorded on a Prestige-21 FT-IR spectrometer (Shimadzu). UV–Vis absorption spectrums were obtained on 8453 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies). Photoluminescence (PL) spectrums were measured on Hitachi F-4500 spectrophotometer and PL lifetimes were measured using FLSP920 lifetime and steady state spectrometer (Edinburgh).

The CDs were synthesized through a simple one-pot solvothermal method. In brief, 0.4477 g of oPD and 15.00 mL of phosphoric acid were added into a 50.00 mL Teflon autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 24 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the dark-brown mixture was purified with a 0.22 μm filter and dialysis membrane. This solution was further diluted to 1 L with ultrapure water and stored at 4 °C for further use.

The control CDs were conducted by using the same reaction conditions as that for oCDs except the exchange of precursors or solvents. The mCDs and pCDs were synthesized by using mPD/pPD to replace oPD, and sCD/wCDs were synthesized by using sulphuric acid or water to replace the phosphoric acid, respectively.

The standard ratiometric assay for Cr(VI) detection was performed at room temperature. A 10.00 mL mixture including 1.00 mL oCDs, 2.00 mL Britton–Robinson buffer (BR buffer. pH = 2.00) and different concentrations of Cr(VI) were incubate in room temperature for 10 mins. After that, the PL spectrum was collected by a fluorescence spectrophotometer with an excitation wavelength of 388 nm.

To study the nature of the prepared CDs, morphologies were first characterized by the transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The particles show quasi-spherical morphology (Fig. 2 a) and the high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image (Fig. 2b) reveals that the oCDs contain a well-resolved lattice spacing of 0.21 nm which corresponds to the graphite-like structure. In Fig. 2c, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of oCDs shows a diffraction peak around 23°, demonstrating the (002) crystal face, which agrees well with the HR-TEM imaging [19].

The optical properties of the oCDs were then explored and the fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum is shown in Fig. 2d. It can be seen the appearance of broad band at 3000 cm−1 belongs to the O–H and N–H stretching vibration and the absorption peak at 1639 cm−1 indicates the existence of functional groups of CO and CN [20]. These results imply the existence of a large amounts of nitrogen/oxygen-containing groups on the surface. In Fig. 2e, the UV–Vis absorption spectrum contains three absorption peaks at 229, 274 and 360 nm. Evidently, the typical peak at 229 nm is ascribed to the π → π* transition of aromatic CC bond, which is consistent with the HR-TEM and XRD results. The other two peaks should be caused by the n → π* transition of the surface states containing CO, CN and other connected groups [21].

Next, The PL properties were characterized and Fig. 3 a represents PL emissions under different excitation wavelength. The emissions are gradually red-shifted along with the increasing of excitation wavelength, which shows a typical excitation-dependent feature [22]. As shown in Fig. 3b, a blue light emission with λmax = 445 nm was first observed under the excitation of 388 nm. Upon addition of Cr(VI), an evidently signal decrease at 445 nm was observed and a concomitant red-shifted emission peak at 565 nm was generated and gradually intensified (Fig. 3b). This significant bathochromic shift of 120 nm on the emission wavelength indicates the formation of a lower energy gap after the coordination between Cr(VI) and oCDs. Meanwhile, the emission color strikingly changed from blue to orange (Fig. 3c). More interestingly, the PL emission signal at 455 nm, 565 nm and their intensity ratio I565/I445 are linearly changed with the additional of Cr(VI) (Fig. 3d), which demonstrates this novel system is able to work as a ratiometric sensor for Cr(VI).

The red-shifted emission of oCDs could be explained in terms of the defects changing after Cr(VI) coordination. Since plenty of amino groups are exposing on the surface of oCDs, Cr(VI) has high possibility to coordinate with these groups and form new defects with the heptatomic ring structure (Fig. 3e). Compared with the original defects, the new defects can both improve the rigidity of the structure and enlarge the π conjugated structure, which could be easier to excite the domain π electron and enable the PL emission to move towards a longer wavelength [23]. Thus, the number of original defects gradually decrease when adding Cr(VI), consequently, the PL emission at 445 nm reduces; on the contrary, after the new defects formatted through coordination, the new peak at 565 nm arises and gradually intensifies.

To verify the formation of new defects and the reliability of our coordination hypothesis, the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of oCDs, Cr(VI) and as-prepared oCDs-Cr(VI) solution were recorded separately. After that, the summed spectrum (oCDs + Cr(VI)) is calculated from the sum of oCDs and Cr(VI) spectrum and further compared with oCDs-Cr(VI) spectrum. As shown in Fig. 4 a, it is notable that the summed spectrum is not comparable with the absorption spectrum of oCDs-Cr(VI), demonstrating the Cr(VI) and oCDs are not simply mixed but coordinated with each other. Moreover, with the additional of Cr(VI), Fig. S1 clearly shows the peak at 280 nm gradually increases. Meanwhile, the absorbance at 360 nm red-shifted to 450 nm and further intensifies, which indicate the formation of larger π conjugated structures.

Fig. 4b shows the PL lifetime changes when Cr(VI) was added from 0 to 100uM. It can be seen the PL lifetime at 445 nm remains stable around 9.60 ns, while the lifetime at 565 nm drops dramatically from 6.85 ns to 1.09 ns, providing a strong evidence that PL mechanism of defects at 565 nm could be different from the defects at 445 nm. From the detailed PL lifetime data show in Table S1, the contribution of τ1 (0.56 ± 0.03 ns) at 565 nm was clearly increased from 0% to 90.11%. Thus, the τ1 (0.56 ± 0.03 ns) is inferred as the PL lifetime of the new defects, which is significantly different with the original one (11 ± 0.67 ns). These results further demonstrate that new defects were generated after coordination.

In order to further explore the coordination mechanism and possible structures of the defects, two control particles mCDs and pCDs were synthesized by using oPD isomer (m-phenylenediamine or p-phenylenediamine) as precursor to replace oPD under the same reaction conditions as that for the oCDs. Under the excitation at 388 nm, the emission band of mCDs and pCDs (both around 500 nm) are totally different with oCDs (Fig. S2), which indicate the different surface defects structures compared with oCDs. In the presence of Cr(VI), it is obviously that there is no red-shifted peak occured and only the quenching phenomenon was observed (Fig. S2). These results convinced us that oPD is the key precursor, and especially the two amino groups at ortho-position of benzene play vital roles in this defects coordination.

According to Song et al., phosphoric acid is an important factor in the formation of dual-emission CDs [9]. To study the effect of doped phosphorus in this coordination, another two control CDs (sCDs and wCDs) were synthesized by using oPD as precursor and sulfuric acid or water to replace phosphoric acid as solvent. As shown in Fig. S3, the PL spectrum of both two control CDs exhibit similar PL emission as oCDs with the maximum PL emission around 445 nm. After coordinating with Cr(VI), the two control CDs show similar phenomenon and also generate red-shifted peaks at 565 nm, indicating the formation of similar defects after the Cr(VI) coordination. Consequently, the two amino groups at ortho-position of benzene, rather than phosphorus, are associated with this defects-related coordination, which have been inferred as parts of the defects.

To further prove the reliability of our hypothesis, time-dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT) was used to calculate the optical properties of mimicked computationally model, called pyrene-based model which has already been widely used for the theoretical studies of CDs [24,25]. Although the absolute values from the model are not directly comparable with the real experiment, the calculated data is able to show the spectrum shift trend after coordination. The optimized geometric structures were obtained at B3LYP/6-311G++(d,p) level and showed in Fig. S4a. Based on the optimized coordination structure, the UV–Vis absorption was calculated, conducted and further compared with the un-coordinated model. As shown in Fig. S4b, the absorption spectrum was significantly red-shifted after Cr(VI) coordination, providing a strong proof that the Cr(VI) coordination can help to create a smaller HOMO-LUMO gap compared with the uncoordinated model.

In order to achieve the optimal performance, the effect of pH value, incubation time and salt concentrations were studied. The pH value is a key factor for CDs analysis, since it can affect the PL properties through the protonation or deprotonation processes. Fig. S5a clearly indicate this proposed assay can be analyzed in acidic condition and the optimal performance achieved when pH = 2.00. Next, the incubation time was optimized. As it can be seen in Fig. S5b, the Is/Ib ratio rises gradually when the incubation time increases from 3 to 15 mins and keep stable for the next 24 h, which clearly shows the rapidity and stability of this assay. Thirdly, the effect of ionic strength was investigated by using different concentrations of NaCl. As shown in Fig. S5c, the signal is quite stable when the NaCl concentration is ranging from 10 µM to 30 mM, which demonstrate that our system is high ionic-strength resistant. This is invaluable for sensing applications in complex, multi-ionic environments.

Under the optimal conditions, a series of Cr(VI) dilutions with varying concentrations were analyzed with this proposed assay. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the PL signal ratio enhanced gradually as the concentration of Cr(VI) increases from 120 nM to 200 µM. There is a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.96) between the I565/I445 ratio and the concentration of Cr(VI). The detection limit was calculated to be 37 nM according to the 3ơ rule, which indicate that the proposed ratiometric approach could meet the requirement of sensitive analysis of Cr(VI) in environmental samples. In addition, we compared the analytical performances of this ratiometric assay with other reported Cr(VI) sensors, including some single signal strategies (Table S2). As we expected, our ratiometric assay shows a good sensitivity and linear range. Compared with the single signal strategy, the Cr(VI) concentration in our approach is calculated from both 445 and 565 nm, which could significantly diminish the interference from analyte-independent factors. Therefore, ratiometric could be the main driving force to achieve the wide linear range and outstanding stability/sensitivity.

In addition to sensitivity, selectivity is another critical property of analytical methods. To test its selectivity, this proposed assay was first challenged with two common oxidants: H2O2 and Fe3+. As shown in Fig. 5b, it is evident that there are no PL spectrum changes or ratio varies compared with the blank sample, indicating the effect of oxidants can be almost ignored in this system. Furthermore, the specificity was tested with different metal cations and anions, including Ag+, Al3+, K+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Hg2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, I−, Br−, F−, C2O4 2−, CO3 2−, SO4 2− and S2− (Fig. 5b). All these samples exhibit similar signal intensities with the blank sample except Cr(VI). These results show strong evidence that this ratiometric assay is highly specific towards Cr(VI) over other interferents, showing great potential for analysis in real samples.

To further study the practicability of this proposed assay, the contents of Cr(VI) were tested in real water samples, including a tap water sample and two river water samples (Table 1 ). According to the calibration curve constructed at the same time, the contents of Cr(VI) in tap water and two river samples were determined to be 50.38 nM, 312.69 nM and 255.38 nM, respectively. To test the accuracy of the proposed assay, 200.00 nM of standard sample was added into the real sample and the additional concentrations were tested to be 197.24 nM, 204.06 nM and 203.16 nM, respectively, with a good recovery ranging from 98.6% to 102.0%, which indicate the high accuracy and reliability of the proposed assay.

In summary, we described a feasible and facile ratiometric sensor for Cr(VI) analysis. The oCDs was prepared by the one-pot solvothermal method and a blue PL emission at 445 nm was observed. Owing to the formation of the improved rigidity and enlarged π conjugated defects structure after Cr(VI) coordination, the emission spectrum significantly shifted from 445 nm to 565 nm. The coordination mechanism behind this phenomenon has been investigated and verified. Based on this coordination, we developed a ratiometric Cr(VI) sensor with high sensitivity, selectivity and accuracy, which is verified to be able to use in real sample analysis. We believe that the present data and method considerable extend the currently knowledge and providing us a novel route in establishing the CDs based ratiometric sensor.

YZ, CL and HM conceived and designed the study. CL, YZ, LS and JZ carried out the studies and performed experiments; YZ, CL, LS, XY, JZ and HM analyzed the data; LS and XY provided advice and technical assistance; YZ, CL, LS and HM wrote the manuscript. All authors read and approved the final manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31860089); Graduate Education Innovation Program of Yan'an University (YCX2020007); PhD start-up fund of Yan'an University (YDBK2020-30) and Research Program for Youth Project in Yan’an University (YDQ2018-17).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.106552.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
